title: Apocalypse World (2nd Edition)
resolution_core: 2d6 + stat; 10+ full hit, 7-9 partial, 6- miss and the MC makes a move (PbtA)

vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.pbta_2d6_tiers: The game that named the engine: every move is 2d6 plus a stat (Cool, Hard, Hot, Sharp, Weird), with 10+ a clean hit, 7-9 a hit with strings attached, and a 6- handing the MC a hard move.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.partial_success: The 7-9 band on every basic move offers reduced choices, ugly bargains, or a worse position — Seize by Force on 7-9 means you take definite harm while taking the thing.
vector_explanations.narrative.authority.success_at_cost_narr: Moves like Go Aggro and Read a Sitch codify the 7-9 result as success with a cost the fiction must honor: the target caves but the MC narrates the price or exposure.
vector_explanations.narrative.authority.fail_forward: A miss never means nothing happens: the MC responds with a move 'as hard as they like' — separate them, take away their stuff, activate their stuff's downside — always advancing the fiction.
vector_explanations.narrative.authority.gm_agenda_principles: The MC plays by a printed agenda (make Apocalypse World seem real, make the characters' lives not boring, play to find out) plus principles like 'barf forth apocalyptica' and a fixed menu of MC moves.
vector_explanations.meta.player_facing.gm_never_rolls: The MC never rolls dice; NPCs and threats act through MC moves triggered by player misses and golden opportunities, keeping all randomness in player hands.
vector_explanations.character.character_creation.playbook_ba: Each player claims a unique playbook — Angel, Battlebabe, Brainer, Chopper, Hardholder, Savvyhead, and so on — a physical booklet holding that archetype's stats, moves, and gear.
vector_explanations.character.identity.playbook_moves: Capability is identity: the Brainer's violation glove and in-brain puppet strings, the Angel's infirmary, the Gunlugger's battle-hardened moves exist only on those playbooks.
vector_explanations.character.progression.highlighted_stats: Each session the PC with the highest Hx and the MC each highlight one of your stats; every roll of a highlighted stat — hit or miss — marks experience toward an improvement.
vector_explanations.social.influence.hx_history: Hx rates your history with every other PC and is rolled to help or interfere with them; when Hx climbs past +3 it resets to +1 and you mark experience, rewarding entanglement.
vector_explanations.resolution.modifiers.help_dice: To help or interfere you roll+Hx with that character; on a hit they take +1 or -2 to their roll, and on a 7-9 you expose yourself to danger, retribution, or cost.
vector_explanations.social.relationships.intimacy_moves: Every playbook carries a sex move that triggers when characters sleep together — the Angel's touch heals, the Battlebabe strips Hx to zero — making intimacy mechanically consequential.
vector_explanations.simulation.environment.psychic_maelstrom: The world's ambient psychic maelstrom is a core setting rule: the Open Your Brain move (roll+Weird) lets anyone commune with it for insight, shaped by the MC's answers.
vector_explanations.politics.factions.front_countdown: The MC builds threats — warlords, grotesques, landscapes, brutes — with countdown clocks whose segments (from 3:00 toward 12:00) fire escalating harm if the PCs don't intervene.
vector_explanations.campaign.management.campaign_fronts: Between sessions the MC maintains a threat map organizing every warlord, gang, and hazard by direction and reach, advancing their agendas so the world moves off-screen.
vector_explanations.character.character_creation.relationshi: Creation ends with a structured Hx round where each playbook dictates history questions ('which of you have I slept with?'), seeding cross-character entanglement before play begins.
vector_explanations.narrative.prompts.leading_questions: The MC is instructed to ask provocative questions and build on the answers — 'What's your best route into the compound, Keeler?' — delegating world detail to players as binding fiction.
vector_explanations.combat.melee.moves: Violence resolves through battle moves with fixed bands: Seize by Force is an exchange of harm where hits buy options (inflict terrible harm, suffer little harm, take definite hold of it).
vector_explanations.combat.damage.wound_track: Harm fills a clock-face countdown: segments from 12:00 to 6:00 heal on their own, but past 9:00 wounds are life-threatening and worsen without care, and the harm move can knock you down or out.
vector_explanations.equipment.gear.weapon_properties_tags: Gear carries tags like 2-harm, close, loud, messy, ap, and hi-tech that adjudicate fictional permissions and MC moves rather than feeding an attack table.
vector_explanations.character.lifecycle.retirement: Advanced improvements let you retire your character to safety, swap to a new playbook, or bring in a second character, making graceful exits an explicit advancement choice.
vector_explanations.economy.barter.abstract_wealth: Wealth is abstract barter (1-barter, 2-barter) — oddments, jingle, and favors — with the book pricing what a month's lifestyle or a gang's loyalty costs in barter units.
vector_explanations.downtime.structure.lifestyle_upkeep_cost: At the start of a session you pay 1-barter for your lifestyle (more to live large); if you can't pay, you start the session hungry, desperate, or in somebody's debt.
vector_explanations.vehicles.operation.vehicle_stats_blocks: Second edition stats vehicles with frame, power, looks, armor, and weakness tags (like 'guzzler' or 'unreliable'), plus battle moves for board-and-ram road war.

title: Dungeon World
resolution_core: 2d6 + ability modifier; 10+ success, 7-9 partial, 6- miss marks XP and the GM moves (PbtA)

vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.pbta_2d6_tiers: Every move rolls 2d6 plus an ability modifier (STR through CHA): 10+ is a full success, 7-9 succeeds with a complication, and 6- triggers a GM move — D&D tropes on the Apocalypse World chassis.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.partial_success: The 7-9 band is where Dungeon World lives: Hack and Slash deals your damage but you take the enemy's attack, Defy Danger offers a worse outcome, hard bargain, or ugly choice.
vector_explanations.narrative.authority.success_at_cost_narr: Partial hits mandate narrated costs — on a 7-9 Spout Lore the GM gives interesting but not necessarily useful truth, and Cast a Spell offers unwelcome attention or losing the spell.
vector_explanations.resolution.metacurrency.xp_on_miss: The signature advancement hook: whenever you roll a 6- you mark XP on the spot, so failure literally pays for your next level and softens the sting of a GM hard move.
vector_explanations.narrative.authority.fail_forward: Misses hand the GM a move from a printed list — reveal an unwelcome truth, use up their resources, separate them — so no roll ever dead-ends into 'nothing happens'.
vector_explanations.narrative.authority.gm_agenda_principles: The GM runs on an agenda (portray a fantastic world, fill the characters' lives with adventure, play to find out what happens) and principles like 'draw maps, leave blanks' and 'ask questions, use the answers'.
vector_explanations.character.character_creation.playbook_ba: Classes are unique playbooks — Fighter, Wizard, Thief, Bard, Cleric, Druid, Paladin, Ranger — one per table, each a booklet of look options, moves, gear, and bonds.
vector_explanations.character.identity.playbook_moves: Class moves are exclusive: only the Fighter has a signature weapon, only the Thief has Trap Expert and poisons, only the Wizard rolls Spout Lore with its ritual magic.
vector_explanations.character.identity.bonds_relationships: Bonds are written sentences tying you to specific PCs ('I have sworn to protect ___'); they start filled from playbook templates and are rolled when you Aid or Interfere.
vector_explanations.character.progression.bonds: At end of session you may resolve a bond that no longer applies, mark XP for it, and write a new one — churning relationships is a literal advancement engine.
vector_explanations.character.identity.alignment: Alignment is a chosen move with a trigger — Good: endanger yourself to protect someone weaker — and fulfilling it is one of the end-of-session XP questions.
vector_explanations.character.progression.experience_points: XP from misses, bonds, alignment, and end-of-session questions accumulates until it exceeds level + 7, when you level up and pick a new class move (levels 1-10).
vector_explanations.simulation.faction.fronts_threats: Threats are organized into fronts, each danger given an impulse, grim portents (a countdown of events), and an impending doom that lands if heroes never interfere.
vector_explanations.campaign.management.campaign_fronts: The GM maintains adventure fronts and slower campaign fronts between sessions, advancing grim portents off-screen so dangers escalate on their own schedule.
vector_explanations.combat.melee.moves: There is no initiative or action economy: Hack and Slash and Volley resolve whole exchanges in fixed bands, and the GM's monsters act through moves rather than rolls.
vector_explanations.combat.damage.die_by_class: Damage dice belong to the class, not the weapon — Fighters deal d10, Clerics d6, Wizards d4 — so a hero's lethality follows the archetype whatever they swing.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.hit_points: HP equals a class base plus Constitution and depletes as attrition with no penalties until 0, when the Last Breath is all that stands between you and Death.
vector_explanations.character.lifecycle.death_and_dying: At 0 HP you take your Last Breath: on 10+ you cheat Death, on 7-9 Death offers a bargain you must take or die, and on a miss your fate is sealed.
vector_explanations.equipment.gear.weapon_properties_tags: Weapons and gear carry tags — close, near, far, messy, forceful, two-handed, n weight — that grant or deny fictional moves instead of granular stat modifiers.
vector_explanations.logistics.encumbrance.weight_based: Every item lists weight, and carrying more than your class Load plus STR score leaves you distracted and encumbered, taking -1 ongoing until you shed the burden.
vector_explanations.equipment.durability.ammo_depletion_abst: Ammo is an abstract stock (ammo 3): choosing 'reduce your ammo' is one of the 7-9 costs on Volley, so quivers empty at dramatic moments rather than per shot.
vector_explanations.logistics.survival.rations: Rations are counted supplies: Make Camp consumes one to heal, and Undertake a Perilous Journey burns rations per day, so provisioning gates wilderness travel.
vector_explanations.exploration.wilderness.journey_roles: Undertake a Perilous Journey assigns trailblazer, scout, and quartermaster roles, each rolling +WIS to shorten the trip, spot danger first, or save rations.
vector_explanations.narrative.prompts.leading_questions: GM principles direct constant leading questions — 'You've fought one of these before, Gregor; what did it cost you?' — whose answers immediately become canon.
vector_explanations.resolution.modifiers.help_dice: Aid or Interfere rolls +Bond with the target: on a hit they take +1 or -2, and on a 7-9 you also expose yourself to danger, retribution, or cost.
vector_explanations.equipment.economy_of_gear.starting_packa: Each class picks from pre-bundled gear lines at creation — the Fighter chooses dungeon rations plus armor options rather than shopping from a price list.
vector_explanations.logistics.maintenance.hireling_upkeep: Hirelings have skills (protector, tracker, priest), a cost that must keep being paid, and a loyalty score; unpaid or mistreated help fails you when it matters.

title: Monsterhearts 2
resolution_core: 2d6 + stat (Hot, Cold, Volatile, Dark); 10+ hit, 7-9 partial, 6- miss (PbtA)

vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.pbta_2d6_tiers: Moves roll 2d6 plus one of four stats — Hot, Cold, Volatile, Dark — with 10+ clean, 7-9 messy, and 6- inviting the MC to make the teenage monster's life worse.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.partial_success: On a 7-9, Turn Someone On gives them a String on you too, and Lash Out Physically costs you exposure — the middle band always tangles you deeper in the mess.
vector_explanations.narrative.authority.success_at_cost_narr: Partial hits are narrated as wins with social fallout: you get what you wanted but hand over leverage, escalate the drama, or reveal something you meant to hide.
vector_explanations.narrative.authority.fail_forward: A miss is a golden opportunity for the MC to make a move — put them together, isolate them, inflict a condition — so failed rolls escalate the melodrama rather than stall it.
vector_explanations.narrative.authority.gm_agenda_principles: The MC follows an agenda (make each character's life not boring, say what honesty demands, play to find out) and principles like addressing yourself to the characters, not the players.
vector_explanations.meta.player_facing.gm_never_rolls: The MC never touches dice; NPCs act only through MC moves and by holding Strings, keeping every roll — and every risk — on the players' side of the table.
vector_explanations.character.character_creation.playbook_ba: Each player takes a unique skin — the Vampire, the Werewolf, the Ghost, the Witch, the Fae, the Queen, the Mortal — a booklet bundling stats, moves, sex move, and Darkest Self.
vector_explanations.character.identity.playbook_moves: Skin moves are the monster metaphor made rules: the Vampire's Hypnotic, the Fae's binding promises, the Witch's hexes exist only on those skins and define what each monster is.
vector_explanations.social.influence.strings_leverage: Strings are the core social currency: gain them by turning someone on, learning secrets, or backstory, then spend a String to add to a roll against them, tempt them with an experience point, or trigger their conditions.
vector_explanations.social.relationships.intimacy_moves: Every skin has a sex move that fires when the character has sex — the Ghost's possession, the Vampire's cold hunger — so intimacy always changes the mechanical landscape.
vector_explanations.mental.composure.emotional_states: Shut Someone Down and social fallout inflict written conditions like 'humiliated' or 'terrified' that color how everyone treats you and take deliberate fictional work — or lashing out — to shake off.
vector_explanations.character.lifecycle.monstrous_transforma: Each skin has a Darkest Self with a trigger and an escape clause: pushed too far, the Werewolf hunts and the Queen turns her clique into a weapon until the fiction buys them back.
vector_explanations.resolution.metacurrency.xp_on_miss: Rolling a 6- marks experience, so the game pays you to keep reaching past your competence; five experience buys an advancement from your skin's list.
vector_explanations.narrative.prompts.leading_questions: The MC is told to ask provocative questions and build on answers — 'why can't you stop thinking about them?' — turning the players' confessions into canon.
vector_explanations.character.character_creation.relationshi: Each skin's backstory section hands out starting Strings ('someone knows what you are: they get 2 Strings on you'), wiring the cast together before the first scene.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.hit_points: Violence is tracked in four harm boxes; most attacks deal 1 harm, and filling the track means death or something stranger, so physical fights stay short and terrifying.
vector_explanations.narrative.tone.genre_emulation_rules: The rules codify messy teen paranormal romance: Turn Someone On works regardless of stated orientation, deliberately modelling the confusing gravity of adolescent desire.

title: Masks: A New Generation
resolution_core: 2d6 + Label (Danger, Freak, Savior, Superior, Mundane); 10+ hit, 7-9 partial (PbtA)

vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.pbta_2d6_tiers: Moves roll 2d6 plus a Label — Danger, Freak, Savior, Superior, or Mundane — with the standard 10+/7-9/6- PbtA bands adjudicating teen superheroics.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.partial_success: On a 7-9, Directly Engage means you trade blows, and Unleash Your Powers means the GM picks a cost — collateral, lost control, or unwanted attention.
vector_explanations.narrative.authority.success_at_cost_narr: Middle-band results are narrated as heroics with a price: you stop the bus but the bridge cracks, you land the punch but your costume tears in front of the cameras.
vector_explanations.narrative.authority.fail_forward: Misses feed the GM's move list — villains escalate, adults condescend, collateral mounts — so failure always pushes the episode toward its next beat.
vector_explanations.narrative.authority.gm_agenda_principles: The GM's agenda (make the world feel like a comic book, make their lives superheroic and their teenage selves complicated) and principles bind every hard move to genre and theme.
vector_explanations.meta.player_facing.gm_never_rolls: The GM rolls nothing; villains hit back through GM moves, conditions, and Influence shifts, so the dice only ever answer for the young heroes.
vector_explanations.character.character_creation.playbook_ba: Each player takes a unique playbook keyed to a superhero coming-of-age arc — the Beacon, the Bull, the Doomed, the Janus, the Legacy, the Nova, and more.
vector_explanations.character.identity.playbook_moves: Playbook moves carry each arc's theme: the Nova's flares and burn, the Doomed's doom track and sanctuary, the Legacy's obligations to a heroic lineage.
vector_explanations.character.identity.values_drives: The five Labels are shifting self-image ratings: moves roll them, and events, mentors, and teammates shift one up and another down, mechanizing who this teen believes they are.
vector_explanations.social.influence.strings_leverage: Influence is held leverage: adults and teammates with Influence over you can shift your Labels or inflict conditions, and you can spend or reject Influence to fight for your own self-definition.
vector_explanations.mental.composure.emotional_states: Harm and social blows land as five conditions — Afraid, Angry, Guilty, Hopeless, Insecure — each cleared only by a specific behavior, like Angry demanding you break something or hurt someone.
vector_explanations.resolution.modifiers.condition_penalties: Each marked condition imposes -2 on particular moves (Afraid penalizes directly engaging, Insecure penalizes standing in defense), so feelings mechanically bend behavior.
vector_explanations.cooperation.assistance.shared_resources: The Team pool builds when the team enters battle together behind a leader; anyone can spend Team to add +1 to a teammate's roll, and acting selfishly drains it.
vector_explanations.resolution.metacurrency.xp_on_miss: Missed rolls mark potential, and a filled potential track buys an advancement — so getting knocked around by Halcyon City is literally how sidekicks grow up.
vector_explanations.narrative.tone.trope_invocation: The unlocked Moment of Truth is a one-shot genre trope: the player narrates their hero seizing the spotlight and it simply works, after which one Label locks permanently.
vector_explanations.narrative.tone.genre_emulation_rules: The system codifies teen-superhero fiction: no hit points, only feelings and self-image; adults reshape you through Influence; Halcyon City's four generations of heroes frame every arc.
vector_explanations.character.character_creation.relationshi: Setup runs 'when our team first came together' questions plus assigning starting Influence and relationships, so the team's shared history exists before issue #1.
vector_explanations.narrative.prompts.leading_questions: Playbook backstory questions and GM prompts ('who in your family do you fear disappointing?') put character truths on the table as binding fiction.
vector_explanations.combat.melee.moves: Fights use fixed-band moves — Directly Engage a Threat trades blows and options, Take a Powerful Blow rolls to see whether you're knocked into next week or lash back.
vector_explanations.character.lifecycle.retirement: Late advancements include retiring from the life or becoming a paragon of the city, plus taking adult moves — the mechanical end of adolescence.

title: Urban Shadows
resolution_core: 2d6 + stat; 10+ hit, 7-9 partial, 6- miss and the MC moves (PbtA)

vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.pbta_2d6_tiers: Moves roll 2d6 plus a stat (Blood, Heart, Mind, Spirit), with the classic 10+/7-9/6- bands driving political urban fantasy in a haunted modern city.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.partial_success: The 7-9 band keeps the city's ledger balanced: you get what you came for, but you owe a Debt, expose a weakness, or attract a faction's attention.
vector_explanations.narrative.authority.success_at_cost_narr: Partial hits are narrated as wins that cost standing or freedom — the ghost talks, but now the Court of Night knows exactly where you drink.
vector_explanations.narrative.authority.fail_forward: Misses hand the MC a move from the list — put them in a spot, activate a faction's agenda, call in a Debt — so failure tightens the web instead of pausing it.
vector_explanations.narrative.authority.gm_agenda_principles: The MC plays to an agenda (keep the city alive and dark, fill the characters' lives with drama and danger, play to find out) with principles that treat the city itself as a character.
vector_explanations.meta.player_facing.gm_never_rolls: The MC never rolls; NPCs and factions act through MC moves, Debts, and corruption temptations, leaving all dice — and all risk — with the players.
vector_explanations.character.character_creation.playbook_ba: Archetypes are unique playbooks sorted by faction: the Aware, Hunter, and Veteran for Mortality; the Vamp, Wolf, and Spectre for Night; the Wizard and Oracle for Power; the Fae and Tainted for Wild.
vector_explanations.character.identity.playbook_moves: Each playbook's moves carry its monster-politics niche — the Vamp's web of servitude, the Oracle's visions, the Tainted's bargain with its dark patron.
vector_explanations.social.influence.favor_debts: Debts are the city's true currency: characters owe and hold explicit Debts, and cashing one in compels a favor, aid, or forgiveness — refusing costs standing and invites reprisal.
vector_explanations.social.reputation.faction_standing: Every character has a rating in each of the four factions — Mortality, Night, Power, Wild — rolled whenever they deal with that faction's members and shifted by play.
vector_explanations.politics.factions.circles: Hit the Street rolls +Faction to locate someone useful in that community: on a 7-9 they'll help, but they want something in return — a Debt, a favor, or your silence.
vector_explanations.campaign.progression.story_xp_triggers: Advancement runs on the faction wheel: you mark a faction when you meaningfully entangle with its members, and marking all four earns an advance and clears the wheel, forcing engagement with the whole city.
vector_explanations.magic.risk.corruption: Each playbook lists corruption triggers — using dark power, breaking your own code — that fill a corruption track; filled tracks force corruption advances with seductive, terrible moves.
vector_explanations.character.lifecycle.monstrous_transforma: Take your fifth corruption advance and the character is lost: they become a Threat under MC control, damnation by increments rather than death.
vector_explanations.campaign.management.campaign_fronts: The MC bundles the city's dangers into storms — brewing crises of linked threats tracked and advanced between sessions until they break over the characters.
vector_explanations.narrative.prompts.leading_questions: The MC asks pointed questions about the city and its powers — 'who did you have to burn to get this territory?' — and the answers become political canon.
vector_explanations.character.character_creation.relationshi: Creation seeds the web: playbook Debt questions establish who owes whom and why before play, so every character starts entangled in the city's economy of favors.
vector_explanations.narrative.tone.genre_emulation_rules: The four-faction structure and Debt economy codify political urban fantasy — every supernatural act is also a transaction, and power always has a lender.

title: Monster of the Week
resolution_core: 2d6 + rating (Charm, Cool, Sharp, Tough, Weird); 10+ hit, 7-9 partial (PbtA)

vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.pbta_2d6_tiers: Hunters roll 2d6 plus a rating — Charm, Cool, Sharp, Tough, or Weird — with 10+ a clean win, 7-9 a win with a catch, and 6- letting the Keeper twist the knife.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.partial_success: On 7-9, Act Under Pressure means the Keeper offers a worse outcome, hard choice, or price, and Protect Someone means you take the harm meant for them.
vector_explanations.narrative.authority.success_at_cost_narr: Middle results narrate monster-hunting TV beats: the shot lands but the creature drags your friend into the dark, the ritual works but something else heard it.
vector_explanations.narrative.authority.fail_forward: Misses trigger Keeper moves — separate the hunters, put innocents in danger, advance the countdown — so botched rolls accelerate the episode's doom clock.
vector_explanations.narrative.authority.gm_agenda_principles: The Keeper runs a printed agenda (make the world seem real, play to see what happens, make the hunters' lives dangerous and scary) plus principles and a move list.
vector_explanations.meta.player_facing.gm_never_rolls: The Keeper never rolls dice; monsters and minions inflict established harm and act through Keeper moves, so all randomness sits with the hunters.
vector_explanations.character.character_creation.playbook_ba: Each hunter takes a unique playbook riffing on a monster-hunting archetype — the Chosen, the Expert, the Flake, the Monstrous, the Mundane, the Professional, the Spooky, the Wronged.
vector_explanations.character.identity.playbook_moves: Playbook moves define each archetype's schtick: the Expert's prepared safehouse, the Spooky's dark-side powers, the Chosen's destiny built from player-picked doom.
vector_explanations.resolution.luck.luck_burn: Each hunter has seven Luck boxes, spent to retroactively turn a roll into a 12 or reduce harm to zero; spent Luck never returns, and an empty track invites the Keeper's darkest plans.
vector_explanations.simulation.investigation.mystery_structu: Each mystery is prepped with a defined monster (with a weakness), minions, bystanders, locations, and a hook, so the hunt has an investigable anatomy under its skin.
vector_explanations.politics.factions.front_countdown: Every mystery carries a six-step countdown — Day, Shadows, Sunset, Dusk, Nightfall, Midnight — of what the monster does if the hunters never show up, and the Keeper advances it when they stall.
vector_explanations.simulation.investigation.clue_spending: Investigate a Mystery converts a 2d6+Sharp roll into hold spent asking questions from a fixed list — 'What can hurt it?', 'Where did it go?' — clues as literal currency.
vector_explanations.combat.melee.moves: Kick Some Ass is a single exchange: on a hit you trade harm with the thing, choosing extras like inflicting terrible harm or suffering less, with no initiative or rounds.
vector_explanations.combat.damage.wound_track: Hunters track seven harm boxes: at 4+ harm you're unstable and wounds worsen over time without treatment, and a full track means dead — mostly.
vector_explanations.equipment.gear.weapon_properties_tags: Gear is defined by tags — a magnum is 3-harm close reload loud, a shotgun 3-harm close messy — governing fiction and harm rather than to-hit math.
vector_explanations.resolution.metacurrency.xp_on_miss: Rolling 6 or less marks experience, and end-of-mystery questions add more; five experience buys an improvement, so botched hunts still season the hunter.
vector_explanations.resolution.modifiers.help_dice: Help Out rolls +Cool: on a hit your assistance grants +1 to the helped roll, and on 7-9 you also expose yourself to trouble, harm, or cost.
vector_explanations.magic.casting.freeform_improvised: Use Magic is a freeform menu — inflict harm, banish, ward, communicate, trap — where the Keeper sets requirements, and 7-9 glitches impose side effects; Big Magic needs rituals and time.
vector_explanations.character.character_creation.relationshi: After picking playbooks the table runs an introductions-and-history round, with each playbook dictating how you know the other hunters before the first mystery.
vector_explanations.narrative.prompts.leading_questions: Keeper principles direct questions at the hunters — 'you've seen sign like this before; what killed the last team?' — folding answers into the mystery's canon.
vector_explanations.narrative.tone.genre_emulation_rules: The whole structure — one monster per mystery, a weakness to uncover, a countdown to midnight — codifies Buffy and Supernatural style episodic monster television.

title: Blades in the Dark
resolution_core: d6 pool, keep highest; 6 full success, 4-5 partial, 1-3 bad outcome, crit on multiple 6s (FitD)

vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.highest_die_poo: Actions roll a pool of d6s equal to the action rating and read only the highest die: 6 succeeds, 4-5 succeeds with consequences, 1-3 goes badly, and two or more 6s crit for increased effect.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.partial_success: The 4-5 band is the engine of Duskwall's misery: you get it done, but the GM levies reduced effect, a complication, harm, or a worse position.
vector_explanations.resolution.modifiers.positioning_effect: Before every roll the table sets position (controlled, risky, desperate) and effect (limited, standard, great), framing exactly how bad failure gets and how much success buys.
vector_explanations.resolution.metacurrency.devils_bargain: Any roll can take a Devil's Bargain: +1d in exchange for a guaranteed complication — ticking a bad clock, offending a faction, leaving evidence — accepted before the dice fall.
vector_explanations.meta.structure_tools.progress_clocks: Segmented 4/6/8-piece clocks track everything from picking a lock to a rival's revenge scheme, making abstract progress and looming danger visible and divisible.
vector_explanations.mental.stress.stress_track: Scoundrels mark stress boxes to push themselves (+1d or +effect for 2 stress), assist teammates, and resist consequences by rolling attributes and paying 6 minus the highest die.
vector_explanations.mental.stress.trauma_conditions: Filling the nine stress boxes means trauma: the character is taken out of the score and gains a permanent condition — Cold, Haunted, Obsessed, Paranoid, Reckless, Soft, Unstable, or Vicious.
vector_explanations.stealth.infiltration.engagement_roll: Scores skip the planning paralysis: pick a plan type and detail (assault, deception, stealth, occult, social, transport), then a single engagement roll cuts straight to the action in whatever position it earns.
vector_explanations.stealth.infiltration.flashback: Preparation is retroactive: pay stress to declare a flashback in which you already bribed the guard or swapped the ledgers, with cost scaled to how improbable the prep is.
vector_explanations.stealth.infiltration.loadout_declaration: You declare only a load level (light, normal, heavy) before a score and reveal specific items from your playbook's list mid-job, trading shopping lists for fictional agility.
vector_explanations.downtime.structure.downtime_phases: Play formally cycles through free play, the score, and downtime, each phase with its own procedures — the crew's life is structured like a heist serial.
vector_explanations.downtime.structure.downtime_action_budge: Each scoundrel gets two downtime activities per cycle (more by spending coin or rep), making recovery, projects, and vice a genuine allocation problem.
vector_explanations.downtime.recovery.stress_relief_vice: Indulging your vice — gambling, luxury, obligation, weird ecstasies — clears stress equal to your lowest attribute roll, tying recovery to a named personal weakness.
vector_explanations.downtime.recovery.vice_overindulgence: Clearing more stress than you had marks overindulgence: you attract trouble, brag about the score, or vanish for weeks — relief that overshoots becomes a complication.
vector_explanations.downtime.structure.entanglements_complic: Every downtime the GM rolls entanglements keyed to heat and wanted level — rivals, arrests, cooperation demands from the Ink Rakes or the Spirit Wardens.
vector_explanations.downtime.structure.reducing_heat: Scores generate heat with the law; the reduce-heat downtime action, bribes, and incarceration are the codified ways to cool the crew off before the Bluecoats close in.
vector_explanations.downtime.projects.long_term_project_cloc: Ambitions beyond a single score — brewing new alchemy, mapping a manor, seducing a magistrate — run as long-term project clocks advanced by downtime rolls.
vector_explanations.downtime.projects.acquiring_assets: The acquire-asset downtime activity rolls the crew's tier to secure gear, cohorts, or services temporarily, with quality pegged to the roll and tier.
vector_explanations.downtime.recovery.healing_downtime: Harm sits in leveled slots that penalize action until you take the recover downtime activity, filling a healing clock to tick injuries down level by level.
vector_explanations.politics.factions.status: The crew holds a -3 to +3 status with every listed faction in Doskvol, shifted by each score's targets and favors, determining who shelters you and who hunts you.
vector_explanations.politics.factions.tier: Tier rates every faction's raw power and sets quality of gear and cohorts; the crew climbs tiers by accumulating rep and paying coin, changing the scale of everything they touch.
vector_explanations.simulation.faction.faction_clocks: Named factions pursue written goals on their own project clocks the GM advances between scores, so the underworld map keeps shifting whether or not the crew engages.
vector_explanations.simulation.faction.turf_war: Crews seize claims — gambling dens, fences, turf — from rivals on a claims map, each claim granting concrete benefits and each seizure a war with its former owner.
vector_explanations.resolution.modifiers.help_dice: A teammate can assist by taking 1 stress to add +1d to your roll, sharing the danger of the action they helped with.
vector_explanations.character.character_creation.playbook_ba: Players pick unique scoundrel playbooks (Cutter, Hound, Leech, Lurk, Slide, Spider, Whisper) and the group picks a crew playbook (Assassins, Bravos, Cult, Hawkers, Shadows, Smugglers).
vector_explanations.campaign.progression.group_advancement: The crew sheet advances as a unit: crew XP buys upgrades like boats, cohorts, and quality, while rep drives tier — progression belongs to the gang, not just its members.
vector_explanations.character.progression.playbook_xp: End-of-session XP triggers are printed per playbook (the Cutter marks for addressing challenges with violence), plus a mark every time you roll a desperate action.
vector_explanations.character.lifecycle.retirement: At four trauma conditions a scoundrel is done — retired, imprisoned, or dead — and your stash determines whether you exit to luxury or the gutter.

title: Scum and Villainy
resolution_core: d6 pool, keep highest; 6 full success, 4-5 partial, 1-3 bad outcome, crit on multiple 6s (FitD)

vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.highest_die_poo: The Forged in the Dark engine in space: roll d6s equal to your action rating, keep the highest — 6 succeeds, 4-5 succeeds with consequences, 1-3 fails, multiple 6s crit.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.partial_success: The 4-5 band pays for every job in trouble: the hack works but the alarm trips, the smuggling hatch holds but the Guild inspector wants a cut.
vector_explanations.resolution.modifiers.positioning_effect: Every action is framed by controlled/risky/desperate position and limited/standard/great effect before the roll, calibrated against gear, scale, and tier.
vector_explanations.resolution.metacurrency.devils_bargain: Devil's Bargains grant +1d in exchange for a guaranteed complication — new heat, a faction slight, a ticking clock — sweetening rolls the way the Procyon sector likes it.
vector_explanations.meta.structure_tools.progress_clocks: Progress clocks track jobs, pursuit by system authorities, ship trouble, and faction schemes, breaking every obstacle into visible filled segments.
vector_explanations.meta.structure_tools.shared_momentum_poo: Gambits are the crew's banked swagger: the pool refills when someone rolls a 6 on a risky action without gambit help, and anyone may spend one for +1d — competence begetting competence.
vector_explanations.mental.stress.stress_track: Spacers mark stress to push themselves, assist crewmates, and resist consequences, with resistance rolls refunding pain in exchange for stress boxes.
vector_explanations.mental.stress.trauma_conditions: Maxing stress inflicts trauma from the FitD list (Cold, Haunted, Obsessed, Paranoid, Reckless, Soft, Unstable, Vicious); four traumas ends the character's flying days.
vector_explanations.stealth.infiltration.engagement_roll: Jobs open with an engagement roll rating the plan's detail, intel, and faction friction, cutting straight to the boarding action already in progress.
vector_explanations.stealth.infiltration.flashback: Flashbacks bought with stress establish that you already bribed the dock official or stashed a blaster in the cargo crawlspace — prep declared exactly when needed.
vector_explanations.stealth.infiltration.loadout_declaration: Crews declare light, normal, or heavy load before a job and reveal specific items from playbook lists mid-action, keeping heists fast and inventories fictional.
vector_explanations.downtime.structure.downtime_phases: Play cycles through free play, the job, and downtime — the same heist-serial rhythm as Blades, retuned for tramp freighters and bounty postings.
vector_explanations.downtime.structure.downtime_action_budge: Each PC gets two downtime activities per cycle to recover, indulge, train, or work projects, with extra actions bought in cred.
vector_explanations.downtime.recovery.stress_relief_vice: Indulging your vice — gambling dens on Mek, luxury, obligation, chemical pleasures — is the codified way to clear stress between jobs, overindulgence risks included.
vector_explanations.downtime.structure.entanglements_complic: After each job the GM rolls entanglements driven by heat and wanted level: bounty hunters, Guild demands, interrogations, and old debts come knocking.
vector_explanations.downtime.structure.reducing_heat: Jobs accrue heat with system authorities and a rising wanted level; laying low, bribes, and taking the fall are the codified pressure valves.
vector_explanations.downtime.projects.long_term_project_cloc: Long-term projects — modding a rig, courting a noble patron, decrypting precursor artifacts — advance on multi-segment clocks through downtime rolls.
vector_explanations.downtime.projects.acquiring_assets: Acquire-asset downtime rolls secure temporary gear, contacts, or services at a quality set by the crew's tier and the roll's result.
vector_explanations.politics.factions.status: The crew tracks -3 to +3 status with the sector's factions — syndicates, the Guilds, system governments, the Hegemony — shifted by every job's winners and losers.
vector_explanations.politics.factions.tier: Tier measures each faction's (and the crew's) scale and quality; climbing tiers upgrades gear quality, cohorts, and how seriously the sector takes your name.
vector_explanations.resolution.modifiers.help_dice: Assisting a crewmate costs 1 stress and adds +1d to their pool, binding the crew's fates together one risky favor at a time.
vector_explanations.character.character_creation.playbook_ba: Players take unique playbooks — Mechanic, Muscle, Mystic, Pilot, Scoundrel, Speaker, Stitch — while the whole table picks one of three ship playbooks that defines the campaign.
vector_explanations.campaign.progression.group_advancement: The ship sheet is the crew sheet: ship XP triggers advance shared upgrades, crew quality, and reputation, so the campaign's real protagonist is the vessel and its name.
vector_explanations.character.progression.playbook_xp: End-of-session XP triggers are printed per playbook (the Pilot marks for flying recklessly and showing off), plus marks for desperate rolls and expressing beliefs or drives.
vector_explanations.simulation.technology.starship_modificat: The three ships — Stardancer smuggler, Cerberus bounty hunter, Firedrake rebel — carry module lists and upgrade boxes (shuttles, weapons pods, smuggling compartments) installed over the campaign.
vector_explanations.logistics.maintenance.repairs: Ship damage marks system boxes that degrade play until repaired; the repair downtime activity and the Mechanic's tinkering restore hull and systems at a cost in cred and time.
vector_explanations.character.lifecycle.retirement: Four trauma retires a spacer — dead, broken, or finally buying that quiet moisture farm — with their stash setting the comfort of the epilogue.

title: Band of Blades
resolution_core: d6 pool, keep highest; 6 full success, 4-5 partial, 1-3 bad outcome (FitD)

vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.highest_die_poo: Missions run on the Forged in the Dark engine: roll your action rating in d6s, keep the highest — 6 succeeds, 4-5 succeeds at cost, 1-3 lets the undead answer.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.partial_success: The 4-5 band grinds the Legion down: you hold the bridge but burn black shot, you carry the wounded out but the rearguard pays for it.
vector_explanations.resolution.modifiers.positioning_effect: Position and effect frame every action against the horrors — desperate position is routine when you're outnumbered by the Cinder King's undead legions.
vector_explanations.resolution.metacurrency.devils_bargain: Devil's Bargains offer +1d for guaranteed grim costs — more corruption exposure, a squadmate endangered, supplies lost on the march.
vector_explanations.meta.structure_tools.progress_clocks: Clocks track mission objectives, pursuit by Broken lieutenants, pressure on the Legion, and time running out on the road to Skydagger Keep.
vector_explanations.mental.stress.stress_track: Legionnaires mark stress to push themselves, assist, and resist the battlefield's consequences; a maxed track breaks a soldier when the Legion can least afford it.
vector_explanations.magic.risk.corruption: Exposure to the Blight — undead ichor, tainted water, sorcery — accumulates corruption; at thresholds soldiers take blight marks and mutations, and too much removes them from the roster entirely.
vector_explanations.cooperation.structure.asymmetric_team_ro: Players double as Legion command: the Commander picks missions, the Marshal assigns squads and personnel, the Quartermaster manages materiel, the Lorekeeper records the Annals, with the Spymaster unlocking later.
vector_explanations.campaign.continuity.troupe_play: Nobody owns one hero: each mission players pick from the Legion roster — expendable Rookies, seasoned Soldiers, and rare Specialists — so the Legion itself is the protagonist.
vector_explanations.campaign.continuity.replacement_characte: Death is constant and codified: fresh Rookies are recruited at liberation stops and promoted to Soldier or Specialist through play, keeping the roster — and the grief — turning over.
vector_explanations.campaign.progression.campaign_arcs: The campaign is a fixed fighting retreat from Ettenmark Fields to Skydagger Keep, with time, pressure, and Legion Morale tracked the whole way and a final score tallying how much of the Legion survived.
vector_explanations.downtime.structure.downtime_phases: Play alternates between the mission phase (one primary mission played out, a secondary resolved by Commander engagement roll) and the campaign phase of role actions back at camp.
vector_explanations.stealth.infiltration.engagement_roll: Missions open with an engagement roll rating the plan, intel from scouts, and Legion condition, dropping the squad into the fight in whatever position it earns.
vector_explanations.stealth.infiltration.flashback: Stress-bought flashbacks let soldiers establish prior preparation — cached powder, a scouted ford, a whispered deal with a Mercy — right when the mission demands it.
vector_explanations.stealth.infiltration.loadout_declaration: Squads declare load levels before deployment and reveal specific kit mid-mission from their lists, with black shot for the undead tracked as a scarce declared resource.
vector_explanations.resolution.modifiers.help_dice: Assists cost 1 stress to add +1d to a squadmate's roll — the mechanical spine of soldiers covering each other in a rout.
vector_explanations.character.character_creation.playbook_ba: Specialist playbooks — Heavy, Medic, Officer, Scout, Sniper — carry unique abilities, while Rookies use a stripped sheet until battlefield promotion earns them one.
vector_explanations.character.progression.playbook_xp: End-of-mission XP triggers are printed per playbook and rank, and desperate rolls mark attribute XP, so veterans are literally forged from bad positions.
vector_explanations.downtime.projects.long_term_project_cloc: Camp projects — alchemical research, Mercies' remedies, the Lorekeeper's chronicles, repairing wagons — advance on long-term clocks during campaign phases.
vector_explanations.downtime.projects.acquiring_assets: The Quartermaster's acquire actions procure horses, wagons, food, and black shot, with quality and quantity set by rolls and the Legion's dwindling resources.
vector_explanations.logistics.survival.supply: The Legion's food, horses, and materiel are tracked stocks the Quartermaster manages; shortfalls on the march mean eating the horses and bleeding Morale.
vector_explanations.simulation.faction.faction_turns: Between missions the Cinder King's forces advance: time passes on the campaign map, the chosen Broken (Blighter, Breaker, or Render) fields its own units, and ignored fronts collapse.
vector_explanations.campaign.progression.group_advancement: The Legion advances as an institution — Morale rises and falls with victories and dead soldiers, and Legion-wide upgrades, alchemists, and Mercies persist beyond any individual.
vector_explanations.narrative.worldbuilding.setting_truths_s: Campaign setup selects among pre-written configurations: which Chosen marches with the Legion (Zora, Shreya, or the Horned One) and which Broken hunt it, reshaping missions and threats.

title: Ironsworn
resolution_core: d6 + stat vs. two d10 challenge dice; beat both = strong hit, one = weak hit, none = miss

vector_explanations.meta.player_facing.player_facing_rolls: Only players roll: every move pits your d6 action die plus stat against two d10 challenge dice, and foes exist purely as fictional dangers and progress tracks.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.partial_success: Beating one challenge die but not both is a weak hit — you succeed with a cost, lesser effect, or troublesome new development, the connective tissue of every quest.
vector_explanations.resolution.metacurrency.momentum: Momentum runs -6 to +10: strong hits bank it, and burning it replaces your action score with your momentum value to convert a miss into a hit, then resets to +2 (lower with debilities).
vector_explanations.character.progression.vows: Play is built on iron vows: Swear an Iron Vow opens a quest with a rank from troublesome to epic, Reach a Milestone marks its progress track, and Fulfill Your Vow rolls that progress to close it.
vector_explanations.campaign.progression.story_xp_triggers: Experience comes only from fulfilling vows (1-5 XP by rank) and forging bonds — advancement is narratively earned, never granted for kills or loot.
vector_explanations.resolution.target_numbers.extended_tests: Progress tracks are ten-box extended tests: each milestone marks ticks scaled by rank (troublesome fills fast, epic crawls), and the final progress roll pits filled boxes against the challenge dice alone.
vector_explanations.narrative.prompts.oracle_tables: Ask the Oracle answers unknowns with yes/no odds from almost certain to small chance, plus action/theme, location, and name tables — the dice play GM whenever no one else does.
vector_explanations.cooperation.structure.solo_mode_design: The book explicitly supports three modes — solo, co-op without a GM, and guided with one — with the same moves and oracles carrying all three.
vector_explanations.narrative.worldbuilding.setting_truths_s: Your Truths assembles the Ironlands before play: for each of 14 categories (the old world, mysticism, beasts, horrors) you pick one of three written truths or write your own.
vector_explanations.character.identity.bonds_relationships: Bonds to people and communities are marked as ticks on a dedicated bond track via the Forge a Bond move, and rolling +bonds aids moves made where you're known.
vector_explanations.character.progression.bonds: Forging a bond grants a reward (mark 1 XP or progress), and your accumulated bond track is what you finally roll when you Write Your Epilogue.
vector_explanations.logistics.travel.undertake_a_journey: Undertake a Journey is a formal procedure: each leg rolls +wits, marks progress by rank, and drains supply on weak hits, until Reach Your Destination converts the track into an arrival roll.
vector_explanations.logistics.survival.supply: Supply (0-5) abstracts food, gear, and provisions, shared across the party in co-op; hitting 0 inflicts the unprepared debility until you resupply.
vector_explanations.logistics.survival.spirit: Spirit (0-5) is morale and will, lost to fear and heartbreak via Endure Stress; at 0 you must Face Desolation and risk the shaken or corrupted debility.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.hit_points: Health (0-5) absorbs harm through Endure Harm; at 0 you must Face Death or accept the wounded debility, keeping physical peril tight and personal.
vector_explanations.combat.initiative.initiative_momentum: Combat initiative is held, not rolled: strong hits keep or seize initiative and weak hits or misses surrender it, gating whether you can Strike or must Clash.
vector_explanations.combat.melee.moves: Fights resolve through moves with fixed bands — Enter the Fray, Strike, Clash, Turn the Tide — with the foe as a rank-scaled progress track ended by an End the Fight progress roll.
vector_explanations.character.lifecycle.death_and_dying: Face Death is its own move when death reaches for you: a strong hit means you cling on, a weak hit trades survival for a permanent price, and a miss carries you into the underworld.
vector_explanations.character.progression.experience_points: XP from vows and bonds is spent directly on asset cards — companions, paths, combat talents, rituals — at 3 XP to acquire and 2 XP to upgrade a card's abilities.
vector_explanations.narrative.structure.epilogues: Write Your Epilogue is a codified retirement move: you roll your bond track's progress to learn whether your ironsworn finds peace among the people they served.

title: Ironsworn: Starforged
resolution_core: d6 + stat vs. two d10 challenge dice; beat both = strong hit, one = weak hit, none = miss

vector_explanations.meta.player_facing.player_facing_rolls: All dice stay with the players: d6 action die plus stat against two d10 challenge dice, while threats — pirates, precursor horrors, failing reactors — never roll back.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.partial_success: Weak hits (beating one challenge die) succeed at a price throughout: React Under Fire holds your position but costs, Ask the Oracle complicates its answers.
vector_explanations.resolution.metacurrency.momentum: Momentum (-6 to +10) banks on strong hits and burns to overwrite a failed action score, the signature push-pull that keeps lone spacefarers alive between systems.
vector_explanations.character.progression.vows: Sworn vows anchor the campaign: each has a rank and progress track, milestones mark it, and Fulfill Your Vow converts filled boxes into resolution and legacy reward.
vector_explanations.campaign.progression.story_xp_triggers: Advancement runs on three legacy tracks — Quests, Bonds, and Discoveries — whose boxes convert to XP, so fulfilling vows, forging connections, and exploring are the only ways to grow.
vector_explanations.resolution.target_numbers.extended_tests: Vows, expeditions, combats, and connections all live on ten-box progress tracks marked by rank, resolved by rolling the track's filled boxes against the challenge dice.
vector_explanations.narrative.prompts.oracle_tables: A vast oracle chapter — action/theme arrays, descriptor/focus, space sightings, settlement troubles, character goals — lets the dice improvise the Forge when no GM exists.
vector_explanations.cooperation.structure.solo_mode_design: Solo, GM-less co-op, and guided play are all first-class modes, with campaign setup, oracles, and moves written to serve a table of one upward.
vector_explanations.narrative.worldbuilding.setting_truths_s: Setup walks through truths for the Forge — the cataclysm, the exodus, communities, iron vows, magic, AI, precursors — choosing among written options or authoring your own.
vector_explanations.character.identity.bonds_relationships: Connections are named NPCs with rank and progress tracks; Make a Connection starts them, Develop Your Relationship marks progress, and Forge a Bond ticks the Bonds legacy.
vector_explanations.logistics.travel.undertake_a_journey: Undertake an Expedition turns spaceflight into legs of rolled waypoints — derelicts, anomalies, hostile patrols — marking progress by rank until you Finish the Expedition.
vector_explanations.logistics.survival.supply: Supply (0-5) abstracts fuel, rations, and spare parts shared by the crew; running dry marks the unprepared impact and makes deep space genuinely lethal.
vector_explanations.logistics.survival.spirit: Spirit (0-5) erodes under isolation and horror through Endure Stress; at 0, Face Desolation can leave lasting impacts on the far side of hope.
vector_explanations.combat.wounds.hit_points: Health (0-5) is worn down via Endure Harm; at 0 you must Face Death, keeping every firefight and vacuum breach personally dangerous.
vector_explanations.combat.initiative.initiative_momentum: Combat alternates between being In Control and In a Bad Spot: outcomes shift your stance, and your stance gates which combat moves are even available.
vector_explanations.combat.melee.moves: Fights use fixed-band moves — Gain Ground, React Under Fire, Strike, Clash — against rank-scaled foe progress tracks closed out by Take Decisive Action.
vector_explanations.character.lifecycle.death_and_dying: Face Death confronts mortality directly, with weak hits buying survival at permanent cost — and the oracles happy to tell you exactly what the void takes.
vector_explanations.character.progression.experience_points: XP earned from legacy tracks buys asset cards — paths, modules, companions, deeds — at 3 XP new and 2 XP upgraded, assembling your spacer piece by piece.
vector_explanations.simulation.astronomy.sector_generation: Campaign setup and play generate sectors procedurally: oracles roll planets by class, settlements, stellar objects, derelicts, and precursor vaults onto your sector map.
vector_explanations.logistics.maintenance.repairs: Your command-vehicle starship has an integrity meter dented by Withstand Damage; the Repair move spends time and supply to patch her back together between crises.

title: City of Mist
resolution_core: 2d6 + power tags (minus weakness tags); 10+ hit, 7-9 partial (PbtA-derived Mist engine)

vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.pbta_2d6_tiers: Core moves roll 2d6 plus Power — the count of your relevant power tags minus weakness tags — read on the familiar 10+/7-9/6- bands of its PbtA ancestry.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.partial_success: The 7-9 band drives noir compromise: Investigate yields Clues but exposes you, Go Toe to Toe wins ground while the Danger hits back with a status.
vector_explanations.narrative.authority.success_at_cost_narr: Partial hits are narrated as success soured by the City: the confession comes, but the Mist stirs and someone upstairs now knows a Rift is asking questions.
vector_explanations.narrative.authority.fail_forward: Misses invite hard MC moves — Dangers act, statuses land, the case twists — so a blown roll deepens the conspiracy rather than stopping the scene.
vector_explanations.resolution.modifiers.tag_based: Everything is tags: power tags add to rolls where they plausibly apply, weakness tags subtract and feed growth, and story tags and statuses layer onto scenes — you can even burn a tag for a guaranteed decisive effect at the cost of scratching it.
vector_explanations.character.character_creation.mythos_logo: Characters are Rifts built from four theme cards split between Mythos (the legend blazing through — Excalibur, Baba Yaga) and Logos (detective, taxi driver), each theme defined by a themebook.
vector_explanations.character.attributes.no_attributes: There are no attribute scores at all: capability is entirely the tags on your theme cards, counted and interpreted fresh for every roll.
vector_explanations.combat.melee.power_tags: Fighting means invoking power tags — 'razor-sharp claws', 'police training' — each relevant tag adding +1 Power to Go Toe to Toe or Hit With All You've Got.
vector_explanations.combat.damage.status_spectrums: All harm and hindrance land as named tiered statuses (bleeding-out-4, terrified-2) on descriptive spectrums; overlapping statuses combine by tier and top tiers take characters out of the scene.
vector_explanations.character.identity.values_drives: Each Mythos theme poses a Mystery and each Logos theme an Identity — statements play repeatedly challenges; betraying them cracks or fades the theme until it is replaced by its opposite kind.
vector_explanations.character.progression.playbook_xp: Growth is printed on the theme cards: invoking a theme's weakness tags marks Attention, and three Attention buys that themebook's listed improvements — while neglect marks crack/fade toward losing the theme.
vector_explanations.simulation.investigation.clue_spending: The Investigate move converts hits into Clues, a spendable currency of questions the MC must answer truthfully, so casework advances through banked revelations.
vector_explanations.simulation.investigation.mystery_structu: MC tools structure cases as icebergs: the visible crime sits atop layered depths of operators and masterminds, with clue trails connecting each stratum of the conspiracy.
vector_explanations.narrative.authority.gm_agenda_principles: The MC follows written principles tuned to noir legend — keep the Mist over ordinary eyes, make the City feel alive, frame the crew's legends against their everyday lives.
vector_explanations.meta.player_facing.gm_never_rolls: The MC rolls nothing: Dangers act through moves and spectrums of statuses, keeping all dice in the hands of the crew of Rifts.
vector_explanations.resolution.modifiers.help_dice: Crew members allocate Help and Hurt points toward each other at creation and spend them to add or subtract from another player's roll in the moment.
vector_explanations.narrative.structure.montage_scenes: Between cases the game runs downtime montages: each player picks an activity — recover, investigate a lead, train, indulge — resolved as compressed framed beats rather than full scenes.

title: Brindlewood Bay
resolution_core: 2d6 + stat; theorize with 2d6 + Clues incorporated minus mystery Complexity (Carved from Brindlewood)

vector_explanations.resolution.core_mechanic.pbta_2d6_tiers: The Mavens' moves roll 2d6 plus a stat (Vitality, Composure, Reason, Presence, Sensitivity) on standard PbtA bands, tuned for elderly amateur sleuths in a Maine seaside town.
vector_explanations.resolution.degrees.partial_success: The 7-9 band keeps mysteries cozy but costly: you get the gossip, the ledger, or the alibi, but a suspect notices, a friendship strains, or night falls first.
vector_explanations.narrative.authority.fail_forward: Misses hand the Keeper a move — the murderer acts, the Void's cult stirs, a Maven's past resurfaces — so failure thickens the plot instead of pausing it.
vector_explanations.narrative.authority.success_at_cost_narr: Partial hits are narrated as small-town wins with strings attached, the casserole-diplomacy version of PbtA's success-at-a-cost tier.
vector_explanations.simulation.investigation.mystery_structu: Each mystery ships with a victim, suspects, a Complexity rating, and a list of discoverable Clues — but deliberately no predetermined culprit for the Mavens to 'find'.
vector_explanations.simulation.investigation.clue_spending: Clues gathered during the investigation are the currency of the finale: the theorize roll adds the number of Clues the Mavens weave into their accusation and subtracts the mystery's Complexity.
vector_explanations.narrative.authority.fact_declaration: The theorize move is shared authorship of truth: on a hit, the theory the players assembled from Clues becomes the actual solution, canonized on the spot — the Keeper never knew whodunit either.
vector_explanations.character.character_creation.clue_questi: Creation and mystery openings run on pointed questions — Mavens define their cozy activity, their pasts, and what they know of victims and suspects — answers that seed the town's secrets and Clues.
vector_explanations.mental.composure.emotional_states: Harm and shocks land as written conditions on the Maven's sheet rather than hit points, penalizing moves where the condition plausibly interferes.
vector_explanations.downtime.recovery.condition_recovery: Each Maven has a named cozy activity — needlepoint, baking, garden gnomes — and engaging in it is the codified way to clear conditions between scenes.
vector_explanations.campaign.progression.campaign_arcs: The campaign interleaves cozy little mysteries with gold crown mysteries tied to the cult of the Void, and accumulating Void Clues darkens the arc toward a climactic final confrontation.
vector_explanations.narrative.tone.genre_emulation_rules: The design codifies Murder, She Wrote drifting into cosmic horror: meddling grandmothers, low-stakes small-town sleuthing, and a slow tidal pull of dread underneath.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.day_night_alternation: Moves are split into Day moves and Night moves: daylight suits interviews and snooping, while the night list is more dangerous, stranger, and closer to the Void.
vector_explanations.narrative.authority.gm_agenda_principles: The Keeper plays to written agendas and principles — keep the town charming and sinister, make the mundane suspicious, test the Mavens' bonds — in the Carved from Brindlewood style.
vector_explanations.meta.player_facing.gm_never_rolls: The Keeper rolls nothing; threats and twists arrive through Keeper moves while every die in the game is cast by a Maven.
vector_explanations.narrative.prompts.leading_questions: The Keeper constantly asks the Mavens leading questions — 'you knew the victim from church; what did she confide in you?' — whose answers immediately become canon and often become Clues.
